sections.header: Verses 1–5 — "I Came to You in Weakness"

heb.paragraph: The noun mystērion (mystery) in v. 1 (some MSS read martyrion, ‘testimony’) denotes not something puzzling but a divine secret now revealed. In the Greco-Roman world, mystēria referred to the secret rites of the mystery religions (e.g., Eleusis, Isis). Paul co-opts the term for the gospel: God’s plan of salvation through a crucified Messiah was hidden in ages past but has now been disclosed through apostolic proclamation and the Spirit’s illumination (vv. 10–16).

The noun apodeixis (demonstration) in v. 4 is a technical term from Greek rhetoric and logic, designating the highest form of proof — a compelling, irrefutable demonstration. Paul’s irony is sharp: his preaching lacked the rhetorical apodeixis the Corinthians valued, yet it came with a superior demonstration — that of the Spirit’s power (pneumatos kai dynameōs). The proof of the gospel is not persuasive eloquence but transformed lives.

panels.ctx: Paul now illustrates the cross-centred epistemology of chapter 1 from his own missionary practice. When he first arrived in Corinth from Athens (Acts 17:15–18:1), he came ‘in weakness with great fear and trembling’ (v. 3). This autobiographical detail may reflect the aftermath of his apparently underwhelming reception in Athens (Acts 17:32–34) or the physical and emotional toll of his missionary travels. Corinth’s rhetorical culture would have made Paul’s lack of polish especially conspicuous; his opponents later mocked his bodily presence as ‘weak’ and his speech as ‘contemptible’ (2 Cor 10:10).

cross.note: Paul’s opponents in Corinth later used his unimpressive appearance and speech as evidence against his apostolic authority.
Paul reminds the Galatians that he first preached to them ‘because of an illness,’ another instance of the gospel mediated through bodily weakness.
The narrative of Paul’s arrival in Corinth, where he worked as a tentmaker with Aquila and Priscilla before beginning his preaching ministry.

notes.note: Paul deliberately resolved to know nothing among the Corinthians except Jesus Christ and him crucified. This was not intellectual limitation but theological focus. Paul was educated, articulate, and capable of sophisticated argument (cf. Romans 9–11), but he refused to let rhetorical skill become the medium of persuasion.

Paul’s weakness, fear, and trembling are not failures but the embodiment of the theology he preaches. A crucified message demands a cruciform messenger. The Spirit’s demonstration of power is visible precisely in the contrast between the messenger’s weakness and the message’s effect.

The purpose statement is crucial: faith must rest on God’s power, not human wisdom. If the Corinthians were converted by rhetorical brilliance, their faith would depend on rhetoric and could be shaken by a more brilliant speaker. Faith founded on the Spirit’s power is unshakeable.

Calvin argues that Paul’s renunciation of eloquent wisdom was deliberate, not accidental. Paul possessed rhetorical skill but chose to suppress it so that the cross would not be obscured. Calvin warns preachers against ‘theatrical display’ that draws attention to the preacher rather than the message.

Calvin observes that the Spirit’s demonstration is inward and effectual: it persuades the conscience, not merely the intellect. The Corinthians’ conversion was proof of the Spirit’s power, not Paul’s eloquence.

The textual variant between mystērion (‘mystery’) and martyrion (‘testimony’) is significant. The stronger external evidence supports mystērion (P46, Sinaiticus, Alexandrinus), which fits the context of hidden wisdom revealed by the Spirit (vv. 7–10).
The phrase en apodeixei pneumatos kai dynameōs employs a hendiadys: ‘a demonstration consisting of the Spirit’s power.’ The Spirit is the agent; power is the mode.

Fee argues that Paul’s autobiographical section is not mere humility but a deliberate theological statement: the form of proclamation must correspond to its content. A message about a crucified Messiah cannot be delivered in the triumphalist mode of sophistic rhetoric. Paul’s weakness embodies the cross.

Fee stresses that the ‘demonstration of the Spirit’s power’ likely refers to visible manifestations — healings, conversions, charismatic phenomena — that accompanied Paul’s preaching. For Fee, the Pentecostal dimension is integral, not incidental.

Thiselton notes that ‘I resolved to know nothing’ (ou gar ekrina ti eidenai) is a deliberative perfect: Paul made a conscious decision about his communicative strategy before arriving in Corinth. This is not anti-intellectualism but a hermeneutical choice about how the cross should be communicated.

Thiselton observes that apodeixis in Aristotelian rhetoric denotes demonstrative proof from accepted premises. Paul co-opts the term to argue that the Spirit provides a superior form of demonstration — one that bypasses rational premises and operates directly on the hearer through divine power.

heb.paragraph: The adjective pneumatikos (spiritual) in vv. 13–15 becomes a key term in 1 Corinthians, appearing 15 times in the letter (compared to only 11 in all other Pauline letters combined). In this context it describes the person who has received God’s Spirit and can therefore discern (anakrinō) spiritual realities that are opaque to the ‘natural person’ (psychikos anthrōpos). The contrast is not between intellectual and mystical but between Spirit-illuminated and merely human cognition.

The adjective psychikos (natural/unspiritual) in v. 14 derives from psychē (soul/life) and designates the person operating solely on the level of natural human capacity, without the illumination of God’s Spirit. It does not mean ‘soulish’ in a Platonic sense but ‘merely human.’ The psychikos person can appreciate human wisdom but cannot receive (dechomai) the things of God’s Spirit because they are spiritually discerned (pneumatikōs anakrinetai).

panels.ctx: Paul now reveals that there is a genuine divine wisdom — but it is accessible only through the Spirit, not through human philosophical inquiry. The language of ‘rulers of this age’ (vv. 6, 8) may refer to human political authorities (Pilate, Caiaphas) who crucified Jesus in ignorance, or to demonic powers operating behind human rulers, or both. The quotation in v. 9 (‘What no eye has seen, what no ear has heard’) draws on Isa 64:4 and Isa 65:17 but is freely adapted, possibly through an intermediate source. Paul’s point is that the content of the gospel exceeds the capacity of unaided human cognition; it must be revealed by the Spirit.

cross.note: The primary source behind Paul’s quotation in v. 9, describing things beyond human perception that God has prepared for those who love him.
Jesus’ promise that the Holy Spirit will teach the disciples ‘all things’ parallels Paul’s teaching on the Spirit’s revelatory function.
The Spirit’s intercession for believers, searching hearts and minds, parallels the Spirit’s searching of the deep things of God described here.
Quoted in v. 16: ‘Who has known the mind of the Lord?’ Paul answers: those who have the Spirit, for ‘we have the mind of Christ.’

notes.note: The ‘rulers of this age’ who crucified Christ did so in ignorance of God’s hidden wisdom. Had they understood God’s plan, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory — a title emphasising Christ’s divine majesty. Their ignorance was not exculpatory but culpable: they suppressed available truth.

Paul’s quotation (v. 9) is often misapplied as referring to heaven. In context, it describes the gospel of the crucified Christ: this is what surpasses human imagination. But God has revealed it — past tense — through the Spirit. The mystery is no longer hidden; it has been disclosed.

The Spirit searches the deep things of God. Just as only a person’s own spirit knows their thoughts, only God’s Spirit knows God’s thoughts. Believers have received this Spirit, enabling them to understand God’s gracious gifts. This is the epistemological basis for all Christian theology.

The ‘person without the Spirit’ (psychikos anthrōpos) does not merely fail to understand spiritual things but actively rejects them as foolishness. The spiritual person, by contrast, makes judgments about all things but is not rightly judged by the unspiritual. Paul clinches the argument with Isa 40:13: ‘We have the mind of Christ.’

Calvin stresses that the wisdom Paul speaks ‘among the mature’ is not a secret gnosis for an elite circle but the same gospel of the cross, now received with deeper comprehension. The mature are those who have moved beyond infancy in faith, not a spiritual aristocracy.

Calvin is careful to avoid a two-tier Christianity: the distinction between pneumatikos and psychikos is between believers and unbelievers, not between ordinary and advanced Christians. The ‘mind of Christ’ is not mystical intuition but the Spirit-given capacity to understand the gospel’s logic.

The phrase ‘Lord of glory’ (ton kyrion tēs doxēs) is a divine title. Its application to the crucified Jesus constitutes one of the most striking christological claims in the NT: the one crucified in shame is the Lord to whom all glory belongs.

The phrase pneumatikois pneumatika synkrinontes is ambiguous: it may mean ‘interpreting spiritual things to spiritual people’ or ‘combining spiritual truths with spiritual words.’ The NET renders it ‘explaining spiritual things to spiritual people.’

Fee insists that the ‘hidden wisdom’ is not a Gnostic-style secret teaching but the cruciform gospel itself, now seen in its full eschatological dimensions. The ‘rulers of this age’ are primarily demonic powers (cf. the apocalyptic framework of 15:24–26), though human rulers served as their instruments.

Fee argues that the pneumatikos/psychikos distinction is ecclesiological, not anthropological: it distinguishes those indwelt by the Spirit (believers) from those without the Spirit (unbelievers). In chapter 3, Paul will introduce a third category — the sarkinos (fleshly) believer who has the Spirit but lives as if they do not.

Thiselton notes that ‘the rulers of this age who are coming to nothing’ (tōn archontōn tou aiōnos toutou tōn katargoumenōn) uses the same verb (katargeō, ‘to render inoperative’) that Paul deployed in 1:28. The cross has already begun to dismantle the world’s power structures.

Thiselton draws on speech-act theory to argue that ‘the person without the Spirit does not accept the things of the Spirit’ describes an illocutionary failure: the words of the gospel reach the unspiritual hearer, but the transformative speech-act misfires because the conditions for reception (the Spirit) are absent.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Faith dominate: the Spirit sovereignly reveals God’s hidden wisdom, and faith — not rhetorical persuasion — is the proper response. Prophecy scores higher here due to the revelatory role of the Spirit. Mission remains strong: Paul’s apostolic method embodies the cross.

rows.label: vv.6–16
rows.text: Spirit-Revealed Wisdom and the Mind of Christ

lit.note: Chapter 2 applies the cross-centred epistemology of chapter 1 in two concentric movements: first to Paul’s own practice (vv. 1–5), then to the Spirit’s role in revealing divine wisdom (vv. 6–16). The argument culminates in the audacious claim: ‘We have the mind of Christ.’

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Paul deliberately resolved to know nothing among the Corinthians except Jesus Christ and him crucified…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Wisdom from the Spirit

translations.text: And so it was with me, brothers and sisters. When I came to you, I did not come with eloquence or human wisdom as I proclaimed to you the testimony about God.
And I, when I came to you, brothers, did not come proclaiming to you the testimony of God with lofty speech or wisdom.
However, as it is written: “What no eye has seen, what no ear has heard, and what no human mind has conceived”— the things God has prepared for those who love him—
But, as it is written, “What no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him”—

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Wisdom from the Spirit within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
